name: calamine (Fast Rust-Based Excel Reader for Python)

performance.notes: Rust-based (calamine crate); 3-10x faster than openpyxl for reading

agent_readiness.notes: Read-only Excel reader (XLSX, XLS, ODS, XLSB) via Rust calamine crate. from python_calamine import CalamineWorkbook. wb = CalamineWorkbook.from_path('file.xlsx'). sheet = wb.get_sheet_by_name('Sheet1'). data = sheet.to_python(). → list of lists. Formats: .xlsx, .xls, .xlsb (binary Excel), .ods (OpenDocument). Pandas integration: import pandas; df = pandas.read_excel('file.xlsx', engine='calamine'). Much faster than openpyxl for reading. No write support. Great for ETL pipelines reading large Excel files.

gotchas: Read-only — cannot write Excel files (use openpyxl or xlsxwriter for writing)
Younger library — fewer features than openpyxl
Binary .xlsb support is a notable advantage over competitors
API is simpler but less feature-rich than openpyxl's full object model
Pandas integration via engine='calamine' is the primary use case
best_when: High-speed Excel reading in ETL/data pipelines
Reading .xlsb (binary Excel) files (rare in other libraries)
When openpyxl is too slow for large .xlsx files
avoid_when: When full Excel feature support is needed (pivot tables, charts, etc.)
summary: calamine (python-calamine) is a Rust-powered Excel reader — 3-10x faster than openpyxl. from python_calamine import CalamineWorkbook; wb = CalamineWorkbook.from_path('file.xlsx'); data = wb.get_sheet_by_name('Sheet1').to_python(). Pandas: pd.read_excel('f.xlsx', engine='calamine'). Reads xlsx/xls/xlsb/ods. Read-only. MIT. Best for ETL reading large Excel files. Not for writing.
